tweets.text:
I get this. At the same time, I imagine the kind of person who decides to marry Naval Ravikant knows that he's not going to be your Average Joe. 

If you fall in love with this person, and they tell you that what makes them happiest is not having a super scheduled life, then you probably want them to be happy. I agree not everyone is going to be down with this, but assuming Naval married someone compatible with him, then she is probably ok with this. (Maybe another reason I am surprised at the pushback on Naval is that I myself have a friend like him! She lives an extremely serendipitous, spontaneous life.)

Also, I didn't actually interpret his statements as saying he never does those things. I interpreted it as that he wants to decide on each one without it being assumed. If it turns out he never does anything with her socially ever, that's a different story. But that's not what I heard. I heard "I want to be super intentional about my time and to not feel obligated with how I spend it." Not "I never want to do anything social."

I'm a weirdo myself in different ways and there are things that my friends know about me that are not like their other friends but they love me anyway, so maybe that's another reason I don't understand why people are so upset.

If the argument is “prohibition can reduce usage” then yes, sometimes it can. But that is only looking at first order effects which is sophomoric analysis.

On the second order effects: if you impose material costs on society in order to obtain the reduction, it cannot be said to have “worked,” for here the cure is worse than the disease.

Prohibition itself—whether of alcohol or other drugs—is the easiest example.

thread_tweets.text:
It's not *necessarily* the case, but is generally the case. And importantly for the topic at hand, it is more often the case to the degree the market is large and complex. That the smaller simpler Singapore example is what you used, when the larger and more complex US situation demonstrates the folly of such prohibition demonstrates this point.

And to bring the point back to AI: the prohibition of advanced GPUs to china has backfired catastrophically. Obviously such a thing cannot be effectively banned (price rises until smuggling is incentivized to solve the demand), and the second order (and more important) effect was to galvanize China's chip industry which went from uncompetitive to now increasingly advanced.

$INTC and $AMD to sign CPU LTAs with Chinese customers for AI DCs (Reuters). 

- Prices of some CPU products have risen more than 40% in China since the start of the year from sources. 

- Month-on-month increases topping 10% for some products

CPUs were already a bottleneck, following CPU ratios due to AI inference... 

But the broader trend of LTAs seems to be appearing from: 

- Memory, with $MU, Samsung, $SNDK, and SK Hynix signing DRAM/NAND LTAs.

- Photonics, with $LITE, $COHR signing EML LTAs. And recent Trendforce reports that $AMD and hyperscalers are now pursuing CW LTAs. 

And I'm sure there's many more from MLCCs to all the way to substrates. 

+1 for the bottleneck investors... hard to be a "bubble that pops" if you have take or pay demand spanning multiple years.

tweets.text:
Just some notes from $TSLA transcripts:

Elon Musk: "I think Optimus will be the biggest product ever" from Q2 earnings transcripts.

Even from Q1, Elon Stated: "I think Optimus will be our biggest product. Not just Tesla's biggest product ever, but probably the biggest product ever.

And I remain convinced of that conclusion". 

It's very rare to see such high conviction reiterated from Elon regarding humanoids both directionally and TAM-wise.

In terms of upstream suppliers opportunities, Karn Budhiraj, VP of $TSLA supply chains stated: 

"We’re seeing the same level of investment going to memory and also new specific items like metal injection molded parts, flexible printed circuits, and all sorts of nonlinear technologies..." 

And that they had to build new supply chains from scratch. So would be interesting to look at for new supply chain supplier opportunities apart from the known ones.  

Just pulled older slides and it looks like capacity targets for Fremont are 1M and Giga Texas was 10M, so at scale, $TSLA seems to be the clear US leader. If you're looking at that S-Curve opportunity ramp. 

On a side note, Elon also gave a shoutout to $MU with likely reasonably priced LTAs, given his statement: 

"We really appreciate Micron making room for Tesla in the years to come and giving us actually a very significant allocation on reasonable terms given the pretty insane pricing of memory these days." (Just for structural memory demand).  

Elon also gave a shoutout to Panasonic which had invested many billions in increasing battery cell production (will need to do deeper DD into Panasonic after this), then Samsung and $TSM. 

Just takeaways from Tesla earnings TLDR: 

Elon tends to be directionally correct on where the future is heading, such as with EV or Space. 

He seems extremely bullish on humanoids.

$GOOGL reported earnings today:

EPS: $9.11 actual vs $2.91 expected - retail reactions to EPS blowout is kinda just accounting noise, since $SPCX, Anthropic, and others were likely large contributors. 

Revenue: $119.7B actual vs. $116.98B expected

Google Cloud Revenue: $24.77B +82% Y/Y, vs. 63-65% expected ($22.2-$22.6B) - this is a very large beat and most material part so far. 

Cloud Operating Income: ~$8.81B, implying 35.6% margin vs. 32.9% Q1. 

Capex: $44.92B vs ~$44.15B (basically in line).

So initial read through is Google Cloud accelerating growth with expanding margins is genuinely bullish for AI demand.

Their former $180-$190B capex guidance is already extremely large, and as long as we get around these numbers in the earnings call + forward projections... 

Should be good to go for $LITE and the other optics/networking trade. (eg. last earnings, they said DC and networking would be ~40% of capex spend). 

Hyperscaler earnings transcripts are probably the most important thing to pay attention to with the AI capex trade. 

And that should be in 3 minutes.

tweets.text:
The economy remains healthy, but rising leverage has left the market increasingly fragile.

With growth holding up and recession risk still low, our cyclical macro regime remains firmly risk-on. The fundamental backdrop still supports equities even as speculative positioning becomes more stretched.

Leveraged ETF assets have reached a record of roughly $218B after rising around 60% since the end of March. Retail options activity is also breaking records, with nearly half of retail options volume now concentrated in 0DTE contracts. Meanwhile, demand for downside protection has faded.

That combination makes the market vulnerable to a sharp volatility event. If momentum breaks, leveraged products would be forced to rebalance and crowded portfolios could begin cutting exposure. An ordinary pullback could become self reinforcing even if the outlook hasn't changed.

This is the volatility event we continue to expect in 2026. If growth holds and credit continues to flow, the selloff would clear excess leverage from an otherwise healthy cycle.

tweets.text:
Just came down from Mt Blanc, we were one of the last groups that went there via Gouter route, now its closed, most guides cancelled and huts are basically empty

https://t.co/gBjIYZysKo

Grand Couloir is a funnel like steep valley you gotta cross to go to the upper Gouter hut before climbing Mt Blanc. This funnel channels rocks falling from the top into a corridor of about 30 meters, you gotta cross. Some of the rocks are size of a fridge and they fall, spin and splinter into smaller ones randomly. The recent heatwaves are melting the ice caps and glaciers that used to hold the rocks together, so now they are falling more than before.

Our guides sent one sentry to the top with a walkie talkie, with the idea we can get a headsup if he sees something move, it takes a rock about 35 seconds to fall, which is about the time you need to cross the gap.

We got clear headsup from the sentry and crossed. Other group was about to go, when our sentry called that rocks are falling. We started shouting to the other group to fall back, and they did, 3 seconds before massive rockfall arrived. I never saw anything like this before.

Our guides told us we are the only one with a lookout, everyone else just crosses early when the temperatures are still low and hopes for the best.

I am quite happy taking huge financial risk, but this is next level.

This is about how that rockfall looked (that woman on the video got super lucky btw):

https://t.co/m8rkokbA0v

Cheap-backed listings get drawn way more often. High-backed listings are rarer pulls. Since the draw is inverse weighted, the acquisition price draws from the expected backing of the pull: the pool’s harmonic mean.

- It sets how likely the NFT is to get selected (less backing = higher chance)
- It acts as a standing buyback offer

A user won a CryptoPunk off a 0.06E spin on FWA today (fun story on that at the end). You may have seen some chatter about the protocol on the timeline.

I’ve been having fun with it and find the mechanic pretty cool, so here’s a quick breakdown of how it actually works.

1/🧵 https://t.co/4QdK3P9X52

5/ Why deposit?

Each successful pull distributes ETH fees across the active listings, so an NFT can earn while it sits in the pool.

The EV on this is roughly neutral, but it also comes with $fwa token emissions, and of course a gambling angle (you could end up EV+ or EV-)

4/ The inverse weighting is the interesting part.

Cheap-backed listings get drawn way more often. High-backed listings are rarer pulls. Since the draw is inverse weighted, the acquisition price draws from the expected backing of the pull: the pool’s harmonic mean.

3/ When you buy a pull, you pay a weighted price based on all NFTs in the pool.

The protocol draws one NFT at random from the live pool.

You can keep the NFT (the depositor gets their ETH back) or accept 85% of the backing (depositor gets their NFT back).

8/ After the initial 15-day emission window, the protocol has a separate fee-funded buyback lever.

A configurable share of protocol fees can be routed to buybacks and, under certain conditions, there is also a per-pull surcharge that is also routed into buybacks.

7/ The $fwa token itself is an additive rewards layer. During bootstrap (first 15 days), active depositors earn emissions weighted by backing, and spinners take home a share of daily rewards. Protocol revenue can also be routed into buying $FWA and back to participants.

6/ FWA also incentivizes large deposits.

The top-backed listing earns an extra cut of fees from each pull. A challenger can take the crown by clearing the incumbent’s backing by a threshold. If the top spot moves or exits, the outgoing holder banks the pot.

11/ The team covered the loss, grabbed an audit, open-sourced the code for public scrutiny, tightened it up, and brought it back.

Credit to @rhynotic for shipping, getting punched, and shipping again.

10/ V2 fixes this by committing the relevant state before randomness is requested. Each pull gets a sequence number and reserves its batch of listings at request time. Inventory is locked from withdrawal while pulls are in flight.

That removes the ability to manipulate the tree.

9/ V1 of FWA was subject to a randomness-timing exploit. Since callback VRF words appeared in the public mempool, an attacker could manipulate the selection tree to shift its weight ranges until the incoming randomness mapped to the exact NFT they wanted.

14/ at 4:45 PM EST, against all odds, the punk is won.

And the winner is none other than @matascup, the person who lost their punk in the original exploit.

So @Rhynotic basically paid the guy 2 punks.

Huge W.

13/ Fast forward to today, V2 is live and another Punk is on the line (this time deposited by @rhynotic).

The punk was backed by 33 ETH among a total pool of 267 ETH. This put its selection chance at 0.00041625%, or 1 in ~250,000.

Everybody (including me) was chasing it.

12/ Now, about that Punk.

You remember that V1 was subject to tree manipulation. Somebody did just that and stole a CryptoPunk from the protocol, which had been deposited by user @matascup.

The team paused the protocol, stepped up, and reimbursed Matas for the loss.

15/ Anyway. I’ve been messing around with it and the loop feels cleaner and more addicting than most of the random NFT things I've tried before.

And it's all onchain.

Congrats to everybody involved.
